perturbation.original_text: 2.13 "Specifications" shall mean the standards established in writing by the Parties for the characteristics, quality, and quality control testing of Product, and its constituents, components, and packaging, as further described in Section 4.4 below and Schedule 2, and as in effect from time to time.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.13 "Specifications" shall mean the general guidelines for product characteristics.
perturbation.explanation: This change significantly alters the definition of 'Specifications'. Initially, it was a well-defined set of standards established in writing and detailed in Schedule 2. Now, it's a vague reference to 'general guidelines,' removing the requirement for written standards and the specific reference to Schedule 2. This omission introduces uncertainty and creates an in-text contradiction because Section 3.6 and other sections refer to concrete 'Specifications' for Batch testing and quality control, but with the new definition, those tests and controls lack a clear, defined basis.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.6 Batch Testing. FUSION shall have the right but not the obligation to conduct any Batch testing [***] or investigation it determines to be of value to determine compliance of Product with the Specifications and/or pursuant to any other standard imposed by law. A Batch shall be deemed to be acceptable if, upon testing, it meets the Specifications, relevant cGMP standards, all other applicable laws, rules and regulations (and any additional tests as agreed by the Parties). If either Party discovers that a Batch does not meet the Specifications, relevant cGMP standards or other applicable laws, rules or regulations, then the discovering Party shall promptly communicate with the other Party. All warranty obligations of CPDC with respect to a particular Batch shall cease and have no effect to the extent that any defect in such Batch arises from abuse, misuse, alteration, mishandling, improper storage or gross negligence by FUSION or FUSION'S employees, representatives, agents, suppliers or carriers, or defects in Precursor materials furnished by FUSION which are used in the production of such Batch.
perturbation.justification: The changed text alters the definition of 'Specifications' from a detailed written standard to 'general guidelines'. This directly contradicts Section 3.6, which requires 'Specifications' to determine compliance and Batch acceptability. The removal of the requirement for written standards and the reference to Schedule 2 in the changed text creates uncertainty about the basis for testing and quality control, rendering the contradicted section unenforceable as it relies on concrete, defined 'Specifications'.

perturbation.original_text: 3.2 Production Orders. From time to time during the Term set forth in Section 6 of this Supply Agreement, FUSION and CPDC will agree to binding Production Orders for Products, as follows: (a) Based on a template order form for such Production Orders provided by CPDC to FUSION, FUSION shall complete the Production Order in such form [***] no later than (a) the order cut-off period for the third-party isotope providers ("Minimum Lead Time"). • In the case of [***] the isotope order cut-off is [***] eastern standard time the week prior to isotope delivery Page 2 of 13 CONFIDENTIAL Supply Agreement - FUSION • In the case of Actinium-225 the isotope order cut-off is [***] eastern standard time the week prior to isotope delivery. (b) CPDC will respond, by email, the acceptance or rejection of each duly completed Production Order within [***] of receipt of such Production Order (an 'Accepted Production Order" or "Rejected Production Order," respectively). If there is no response to a Production Order within such timeline, it shall be considered an Accepted Production Order. In the case of a Rejected Production Order, [***]. (c) FUSION shall be entitled to make changes to Production Orders (a 'Change Order") to alter the quantity of Units packaged or distributed up to [***] prior to the Date of Manufacturing by providing CPDC with written notice [***]. CPDC will respond, by email, the acceptance or rejection of each Change Order within [***] of receipt of such change (an "Accepted Change Order" or "Rejected Change Order," respectively). If there is no response to a Change Order within such timeline, it shall be considered an Accepted Change Order. In the case of a Rejected Change Order, [***]. Changes to the number of Units packaged or distributed with less than [***] notice will be subject to the applicable [***] defined in Schedule 1. Notwithstanding the foregoing, CPDC is unable to alter the total quantity of Product produced after the order deadlines imposed by any third-party isotope supplier. (d) Cancelation of a Production Orders: (i) [***] (ii) All cancellations of Production Orders shall be provided to CPDC by FUSION in writing [***]. (e) [***].
perturbation.changed_text: 3.2 Production Orders. From time to time during the Term set forth in Section 6 of this Supply Agreement, FUSION will inform CPDC of the amount of Products needed to order. The specific amount should be sent over by Fusion, no later than the order cut-off period for the third-party isotope providers. Changes to the amount needed may be sent by Fusion as well, however, CPDC is unable to alter the total quantity of Product produced after the order deadlines imposed by any third-party isotope supplier. All cancellations of Production Orders shall be provided to CPDC by FUSION in writing.
perturbation.explanation: Here, the explicit process for production orders, including acceptance/rejection timelines and change order procedures, is significantly curtailed. By removing sections (a), (b), (c), and (e), and only keeping the beggining of the section, and the tail end, there is an omission of the process around Production Orders. While FUSION still informs CPDC of their needs, the specific order form, response times, and acceptance protocols are all removed. This now contradicts the section where it is described what to do in case of rejections, because now the process of the production orders it not defined at all, but it has been assumed throughout the whole document. Since the specifics of production orders have been removed, it creates ambiguity and uncertainty in enforcement of the 'Accepted Production Order' which the document assumes to exist.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.4 Inability to Supply. In the event that it becomes apparent to CPDC at any time that it will be unable to fulfill any Production Order, then CPDC shall immediately notify FUSION in writing of CPDC's inability to meet such requirements for Product, along with a specific indication of the amount of such shortfall in manufacture of Product and anticipated timing of delivery. If CPDC is unable to fulfill the Production Order or any portion thereof within [***] of the planned delivery, then either: (a) CPDC will [***]; or Page 3 of 13 CONFIDENTIAL Supply Agreement - FUSION (b) CPDC will [***].
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the specific procedures for production orders, including response times, acceptance protocols, and change order mechanisms. This omission directly contradicts section 3.4, which outlines actions CPDC must take if they are unable to fulfill a 'Production Order.' The removed procedures create uncertainty about how a Production Order is accepted or rejected, making it unclear how section 3.4 would be applied or enforced. The original text provided clear steps and timelines; the changed text removes this clarity, creating a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 3.5 Pricing and Payment Terms. Schedule 1 of this Supply Agreement details the pricing and payment terms for the Product. For supply of Product, CPDC shall invoice FUSION [***]. CPDC shall send such invoices to: Address: 270 Longwood Rd. South Hamilton, Ontario L8P 0J6 Canada [***] FUSION shall pay such invoice within [***] of receipt of the invoice in accordance with the Supply Agreement
perturbation.changed_text: 3.5 Pricing and Payment Terms. For supply of Product, CPDC shall invoice FUSION [***]. CPDC shall send such invoices to: Address: 270 Longwood Rd. South Hamilton, Ontario L8P 0J6 Canada [***] FUSION shall pay such invoice in accordance with the Supply Agreement
perturbation.explanation: By removing the first sentence, "Schedule 1 of this Supply Agreement details the pricing and payment terms for the Product", the document now has an in-text contradiction because it's still referencing the 'payment terms' without making clear where these terms are. Previously, the payment terms were defined in Schedule 1. Now, there is no mention of a specific pricing agreement, which the document assumes to exists later on.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 7.4 Termination by CPDC, For Cause. CPDC may terminate this Agreement immediately on written notice to FUSION if: (a) FUSION fails to pay or dispute any invoice in accordance with Section 3.5 and fails to remedy such breach within [***] of a notice from CPDC requiring FUSION to remedy the same and stipulating that FUSION is in breach of this Agreement; or (b) FUSION becomes insolvent or goes into administration, receivership or liquidation or enters into any arrangement or composition with its creditors.
perturbation.justification: The removal of the sentence "Schedule 1 of this Supply Agreement details the pricing and payment terms for the Product" directly contradicts section 7.4, which references Section 3.5 regarding invoice payments. Without specifying where the payment terms are located, it creates ambiguity and uncertainty on how FUSION can pay invoices 'in accordance with Section 3.5' as referenced in Section 7.4, making the 'Termination by CPDC, For Cause' clause potentially unenforceable. This falls under the 'Omissions – In Text Contradiction' definition.
